nativeRange.habitat: Marshy environments, wet stream margins and seepage swamps along the coastal plain of the southeastern United States (Florida through Texas and Georgia) and similar low-elevation wetlands throughout the Caribbean, Central America, and northern South America

decorPreferences: regular trimming to promote denser bushy growth
open midground placement with room to creep

summary: A New World mint-family creeper with small round leaves and a distinctive minty scent when crushed; an easy, undemanding stem plant that works as a foreground/midground creeper or grows tall enough for the background under stronger light. Tolerates a wide range of water parameters and grows without CO2.
careNotes: Mint Charlie (Micromeria brownei, more recently classified Clinopodium brownei) is a sprawling herbaceous mint in family Lamiaceae, order Lamiales, native to wet marshy habitats across the southeastern United States (notably Florida and the Gulf coastal plain), the Caribbean, Mexico, Central America, and northern South America. It is one of the only true Lamiaceae regularly sold for fully submersed aquarium culture, and is distinctive among aquatic plants for producing a fresh, minty fragrance when its leaves or stems are crushed or trimmed. Sources converge on a temperature range around 18-28 °C (Modern Aquarium cites 65-78 °F / ~18-26 °C; Aquatic Motiv cites 75-82 °F / ~24-28 °C — averaged here for a broad 18-28 °C window). Aquatic Motiv reports pH 5.8-7.6, which agrees with the plant's reputation for tolerating soft acidic to moderately hard slightly alkaline water; gH/kH values are estimated as typical for a wide-tolerance Lamiaceae creeper (gH 2-15, kH 2-10). Submersed height is highly plastic: trimmed specimens hold at roughly 7-15 cm (3-6 in) as a foreground/midground creeper, while untrimmed plants in stronger light grow as tall background stems up to ~50 cm (20 in) per Aquatic Motiv. Lighting is flexible: Modern Aquarium notes it 'does not require high-intensity lighting to do well', Aquatic Motiv lists low light, and Aquarium Plants Factory describes low-to-high tolerance — recorded as 'low' for the entry's minimum but growth and density both improve markedly with medium lighting. CO2 is not required (Modern Aquarium, Aquatic Motiv, APF all agree), but supplementation produces denser, faster growth and is recorded as 'optional'. Growth rate is medium-to-fast under typical hobby conditions. The plant is not particular about substrate (APF notes substrate is 'not required'), but performs noticeably better in loosely packed, nutrient-rich substrate with a simple liquid-fertilizer routine, as suggested by Modern Aquarium. Mint Charlie spreads horizontally as a creeping stem plant and benefits from regular pruning to encourage a denser, bushier habit; left untrimmed it will grow vertically. It adapts well to both submerged and emersed growth, making it forgiving for paludariums and ripariums. Note: one vendor (Aquatic Motiv) lists the native range as 'Asia' — this is a data error contradicted by every botanical source (Wikipedia, USDA, NatureServe) which place the species firmly in the Americas; entry uses the correct New World range.
breedingNotes: Propagation is vegetative and very simple: snip a healthy stem segment and replant it in the substrate, where it will root within days. Regular trimming of terminal stems also encourages lateral branching and a denser cushion form. Floating cuttings will develop roots that can then be anchored into the substrate. Emersed specimens may flower (small bilabiate pinkish-white to lavender flowers typical of the mint family) but seed propagation is essentially unknown in the aquarium hobby.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Primary product page. Provided: temperature 65-78 °F, native range Central and South Florida extending into southern Georgia and Gulf-region waters, easy care, moderate lighting ('does not require high-intensity lighting to do well'), CO2 not required, fast growth, midground-to-background placement, prefers loosely packed nutrient-rich substrate, simple liquid-fertilizer routine recommended, propagated via trimming with regular pruning encouraging denser bushier growth.

additional.name: Aquatic Motiv
additional.notes: Provided numeric values: temperature 75-82 °F, pH 5.8-7.6, tank minimum 15 gallons (~57 L), care level 'very easy', lighting low, CO2 not required, maximum height 20 in (~50 cm), background placement, propagation via stem cuttings replanted in substrate. Note: this page lists native range as 'Asia' — this is a vendor data error and is contradicted by every botanical source; the entry uses the correct New World range from Wikipedia/NatureServe.

additional.name: Aquarium Plants Factory
additional.notes: Cross-checked: family Lamiaceae, origin 'North and South America', lighting tolerance 'low to high', CO2 not required but benefits from supplementation, growth rate medium-to-fast, propagation by stem cuttings, substrate not required, fresh mint scent when crushed or trimmed, small round coin-shaped leaves, adapts well to both submerged and emersed growth. Confirms the current accepted name Clinopodium brownei (former Micromeria brownei).

additional.name: Wikipedia (Clinopodium brownei)
additional.notes: Used for authoritative taxonomy and native range: family Lamiaceae, order Lamiales, perennial herb with sprawling square stems and opposite leaves, bilabiate corolla in pinkish-white to lavender, common name Browne's savory, native range 'marshy environments along the coastal plain of the southeastern United States, specifically from Texas through South Carolina' plus Mexico, Central America, South America, and the West Indies. Conservation status secure (NatureServe). Confirms the species was formerly placed in Micromeria and has been transferred to Clinopodium.
